content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
December 28,
1993
Sandi Sigurdson
665 Buchon Street
San Luis Obispo,
CA
93401
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1-93—452
Dear Mr.
Sigurdson:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice
regarding your responsibilities as a planning commissioner for the
City of San Luis Obispo pursuant to the conflict-of—interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”) •1
Since your
advice request does not refer to a specific governmental decision,
we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.
2
-
Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.
In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
71.)
OUESTION
May you participate in decisions of the City of San Luis
Obispo Planning Commission regarding the approval of construction
projects conditioned on the applicant’s submission of a waste
management plan, if such decisions may affect your employer,
a
nonprofit organization,
or your spouse,
a construction waste plan
planner?
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000
— 18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1—93—452
Page
2
CONCLUSION
You may not participate in any governmental decision which
will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
you, your spouse, or any of your other economic interests,
including your employer, your spouse’s business,
and clients of
your spouse’s business, either directly or indirectly.
FACTS
You were appointed to the San Luis Obispo City Planning
Commission in July 1993.
The City of San Luis Obispo does not
have an ordinance or policy which requires construction waste
management plans on construction sites.
However, in recent
months, the San Luis Obispo Planning Commission has been approving
projects with the condition that such projects include such waste
plans.
You are also a salaried business manager for the
Environmental Center of San Luis Obispo
(“ECOSLO9,
a recycling
and environmental education nonprofit organization.
One of the
materials recycled by your employer is scrap metal,
a frequent
waste material generated on construction sites.
Scrap metal sales
constituted approximately four percent of your employer’s income
in 1992.
Increased scrap metal recycling may benefit your
employer, or depress the value of scrap metal.
In addition, your spouse is a licensed general building
contractor, currently under contract with the City of San Luis
Obispo in the amount of $4,000.
Your spouse’s contract provides
that he is to provide a detailed construction waste analysis and a
list of material diversion opportunities for a prominent office
building project.
Your spouse is the only contractor offering
this service in San Luis Obispo,
and in your opinion, he has
developed a statewide reputation for his knowledge in construction
waste management and resource efficient building practices.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
As a planning commissioner
for the City of San Luis Obispo,
you are a public official under
the Act.
(Section 82048.)
Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a


Our File No.
1—93—452
Page
3
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
3
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000) or more.
****
(C)
Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
****
For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official,
by an agent on behalf of a
public official, or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official’s agents,
spouse,
and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.
Section 87103(a)
and (c).
The Commission has adopted differing guidelines to determine
whether the effect of a decision is material, based on the
specific circumstances of each decision.
For example, Regulation
18702.1
(copy enclosed) provides that where a source of income is
directly before your agency as an applicant or the subject of the
decision, the effect of the decision is deemed to be material and
disqualification is required.
wliettier the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on
the facts of each particular decision.
An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that
it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.
(fl
re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)


Our File No.
1—93—452
Page 4
Regulation 18702.1 also provides that an official’s interests
are directly involved in a decision and disqualification is
required where there is a nexus between the purpose for which an
official receives income and a governmental decision.
A “nexus”
exists if the official receives income to achieve a goal or
purposes which would be achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by
the governmental decision.
In other words, you may not accomplish
in your public capacity what you are paid to accomplish in your
private capacity.
(Regulation 18702.1(d); Spraque Advice Letter,
No.
1—88—190.)
Finally, Regulation 18702.1(a) (4) provides the effect of a
decision is material if the decision will result in the personal
expenses,
income, assets or liabilities of the official or his or
her immediate fanily increasing or decreasing by $250.
You are also required to disqualify yourself where a source
of income will be indirectly affected by a decision.
Regulations
18702.2
- 18702.6 provide differing standards for making this
determination (copies enclosed).
Economic Interests
Employer:
Your employer is a disqualifying source of income to
you.
In addition,
any person or business which has been a source
of income to you of $250 or more, is a disqualifying source of
income.
If a source of income of yours is directly affected by a
governmental decision, Regulation 18702.1 discussed above provides
the appropriate standard for determining materiality.
However,
the city planning decisions in question do not appear to directly
involve your employer.
When a nonprofit entity is indirectly affected by a decision,
the standard for determining materiality is that of Regulation
18702.5.
For example, if the nonprofit has gross annual receipts
of $100,000 or less,
3 the effect of a decision will be material
if:
(1)
The decision will result in an increase
or decrease of the entity’s gross annual receipts
for a fiscal year in the amount of $10,000 or more.
(2)
The decision will cause the entity to
incur or avoid additional expenses or to reduce or
eliminate existing expenses for a fiscal year in
the amount of $2,500 or more.
Of course, if the nonprofit’s annual receipts were greater than
$100,000,
other provisions of Regulation 18702.5(f)
would apply.


Our File No.
1—93—452
Page 5
(3)
The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of the entity’s assets or
liabilities in the amount of $10,000 or more.
Regulation 18702.5(f).
Thus, your disqualification would be required, for example,
if the nonprofit entity’s revenues would be indirectly affected by
planning commission decisions regarding a proposed waste
management plan ordinance or policy.
Spouse:
Compensation your spouse receives is also a disqualifying
source of income to you.
Section 82030 provides that the income of an individual also
includes any community property interest in the income of a
spouse.
Therefore,
income to your spouse of $500 or more
(making
your community property interest $250) within the past 12 months,
is a potentially disqualifying economic interest as described in
Section 87103.
As noted earlier, if a source of income of yours will be
directly affected by a governmental decision, Regulation 18702.1
provides the appropriate standard for determining materiality.
Regulations 18702.2
- 18702.6, discussed earlier, provide the
standards for determining materiality where a source of income is
indirectly affected by a governmental decision.
Spouse’s Business:
Presumably you have an indirect investment
interest in your spouse’s business in the amount of at least
$1,000.
Therefore,
your spouse’s business is a disqualifying
economic interest of yours.
(Section 87103 (a).)
Your spouse is a licensed general building contractor
currently under contract with the City of San Luis Obispo in an
amount which exceeds $500.
From your facts, it appears that your
spouse is an independent contractor, not an employee of the city,
or otherwise a public official,
of the agency as a result of the
contract in question.
Therefore, the compensation he receives
from the city is also potentially disqualifying “income”
4 within
the meaning of Section 87103 (c).
Salary received from a state, local, or federal government
agency is expressly exempted from the definition of “income” for
purposes of the Act.
(Section 82030(b) (2).)
However, as a
general rule,
an agency payment constitutes salary which is
exempted from the definition of “income” under the Act only where
the recipient is an employee, or is otherwise a public official,
of the agency.
(Bohicrian Advice Letter,
No.
1-92—280.)


Our File No.
1—93—452
Page
6
To determine whether a governmental decision will have a
reasonably foreseeable
5 material financial effect on your spouse’s
business, you will also need to apply Regulation 18702.1 where the
business will be directly affected by a governmental decision.
Where the business will be indirectly affected by a decision, it
would appear that for a relatively small business entity such as
your spouse’s business, Regulation 18702.2(g)
would apply.
It
provides that the effect of a decision is material if the decision
will result in an increase or decrease in its gross revenues for a
fiscal year, the value of assets or liabilities by $10,000 or
more,
or will result in the company incurring or avoiding
additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses
for a fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more.
Clients:
The city,
a client of your spouse’s business,
6 and other
clients of your spouse are also disqualifying sources of income to
you.
Thus,
you also may not participate in any governmental
decision which will have a foreseeable and material financial
effect on the city or other clients of your spouse.
If you determine that a decision before the planning
commission will directly affect a client of your spouse which is a
source of income to you,
you will also need to apply Regulation
18702.1 discussed above.
With respect to a public agency,
such as
the city,
which is indirectly affected by a governmental decision,
we have applied Regulation 18702.5.
With respect to other clients
which are indirectly affected by a governmental decision,
you will
need to apply Regulations 18702.2
- 18702.6.
The “Public Generally” Exception
An official may still participate in the decision if the
effect on the official’s economic interest is not distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally.
For the “public
generally” exception to apply, the decision must affect the
official’s economic interests in substantially the same manner as
Your spouse is currently the only contractor who can provide
this service in the city.
Thus, it is foreseeable that the
decision will financially affect your spouse’s business.
In
addition, the general rule set forth in In re Thorner,
supra, is
that where the business entity in which the official has an
economic interest makes a bid on a contract or is preparing to
make a bid,
a financial effect on the business entity is
reasonably foreseeable even if there is substantial competition.
Thus, to the extent that your spouse intends to make future bids,
the existence of other contractors who may provide such service
would not change this conclusion.
6
Your spouse has an ownership interest in the business.
Therefore, the clients of the business are also considered sources
of income to you.


Our File No.
1—93—452
Page 7
it will affect a significant segment of the jurisdiction.
(Regulation 18703
(copy enclosed); In re Owen
(1976)
2
FPPC Ops.
77.)
Please note that we have advised that if a decision affects a
public agency,
we generally presume that the effect of a decision
flows to all residents, or a significant segment, in the
jurisdiction in question.
Under such circumstances, the effect of
the decision would be the sane as the effect on the “public
generally.”
(Douglas Advice Letter, No. I-90-128A.)
Therefore,
if the effect of the decisions relating to the conditioning of
waste plans flows to all, or a significant segment, -of the
residents of the City of San Luis Obispo, you will not be required
to disqualify yourself from participating in planning commission
decisions concerning the waste plans as they relate to the city.
However,
you will need to apply Regulation 18703 separately with
respect to the effect on your employer, your spouse’s business,
and other clients of your spouse’s business.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322-5901.
Sincerely,
Steven C.
Churchwell
General Counsel
-
By:
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel, Legal Division
Enclosures
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10c per page.

QUESTION: May you participate in decisions of the City of San Luis
Obispo Planning Commission regarding the approval of construction
projects conditioned on the applicant’s submission of a waste
management plan, if such decisions may affect your employer,
a
nonprofit organization,
or your spouse,
a construction waste plan
planner?
; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1—93—452

CONCLUSION: You may not participate in any governmental decision which
will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
you, your spouse, or any of your other economic interests,
including your employer, your spouse’s business,
and clients of
your spouse’s business, either directly or indirectly.
embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission December 28, 1993 Sandi Sigurdson 665 Buchon Street San Luis Obispo, CA 93401 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. 1-93—452 Dear Mr. Sigurdson: This is in response to your letter requesting advice regarding your responsibilities as a planning commissioner for the City of San Luis Obispo pursuant to the conflict-of—interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”) •1 Since your advice request does not refer to a specific governmental decision, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance. 2 - Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) OUESTION May you participate in decisions of the City of San Luis Obispo Planning Commission regarding the approval of construction projects conditioned on the applicant’s submission of a waste management plan, if such decisions may affect your employer, a nonprofit organization, or your spouse, a construction waste plan planner? 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections 18000 — 18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).) 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 Our File No. 1—93—452 Page 2 CONCLUSION You may not participate in any governmental decision which will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on you, your spouse, or any of your other economic interests, including your employer, your spouse’s business, and clients of your spouse’s business, either directly or indirectly. FACTS You were appointed to the San Luis Obispo City Planning Commission in July 1993. The City of San Luis Obispo does not have an ordinance or policy which requires construction waste management plans on construction sites.
